repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
:backlog:

## Problem

`nvim_exec_autocmds({ buf = ... })` already uses the passed buffer for (`<abuf>`, buffer-local autocommands, etc.), but it doesn't execute *in* that buffer's context.

A minimal example explains it best:

```lua
local target = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)
local caller = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, true)

vim.api.nvim_set_current_buf(caller)
vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd('SomeEvents', {
  buffer = target,
  callback = function()
    print(vim.fn.expand('<abuf>'), vim.api.nvim_get_current_buf())
  end,
})

vim.api.nvim_exec_autocmds('SomeEvent', { buf = target })

-- (<abuf> == target) != (current_buf == caller)
```

Fixes #35393.

## Solution

The tempting & suggested fix is to wrap w/ `aucmd_prepbuf()` / `aucmd_restbuf()` but this is not right. Consider the following example: 

```lua
local target = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, false)
local caller = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(false, false)

vim.api.nvim_set_current_buf(caller)
vim.cmd.split()
vim.api.nvim_set_current_buf(target)
local left = vim.api.nvim_get_current_win()
vim.cmd.vsplit()
local right = vim.api.nvim_get_current_win()
vim.cmd.wincmd('j')
vim.api.nvim_set_current_buf(caller)

local chosen = vim.api.nvim_buf_call(target, function()
  return vim.api.nvim_get_current_win()
end)
local other = chosen == left and right or left

vim.wo[chosen].eventignorewin = 'CursorHold'
vim.wo[other].eventignorewin = ''

vim.g.fired = 0
vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd('CursorHold', {
  buffer = target,
  callback = function() vim.g.fired = vim.g.fired + 1 end,
})

vim.api.nvim_exec_autocmds('CursorHold', { buf = target })
print(vim.g.fired)  -- 1
```

Here the target buffer is visible in two windows `left` and `right` but only one of them ignores `CursorHold` via `eventignorewin`. Currently, the full window set is seen and the event fires (one window allows it). With the erroneous fix, `aucmd_prepbuf()` picks a window up front and doesn't ask all windows whether it is allowed, which can incorrectly suppress the autocmd.

Instead, add a separate buffer-context path for buffer-specific `nvim_exec_autocmds()` and delay the context switch until dispatch executes. This preserves the described semantics (callbacks, modelines run in the buffer too).

***NOTE***: I am not a C dev. This is the most C that I have written in the last year. It is also the most complex issue I have fixed so far so I'm almost certainly missing some things.

comment_data.body: better name? should this even be declared lol

i tried to strip these tests down but modelines and the `eventignorewin` example seemed quite important to keep, as there are many edge cases in this. open to removing, changing, combining. could've explored existing test suite more...
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1205,6 +1201,116 @@ describe('autocmd api', function()
       eq(1, api.nvim_get_var('buffer_executed'))
     end)
 
+    it('executes in the passed buffer context and restores the caller buffer', function()

comment_data.body: forward declarations are autogenerated, don't need this.

so it does not execute in the buffer context under some conditions? what would a doc update look like ? 

(can edit the docstring on this function, and run `make doc` to regenerate docs)
maybe off topic, but looking at `nvim_buf_call`, i'm wondering why `TRY_WRAP` isn't used here...

I assume this is a workaround for the `'eiw'` complexity?
But I feel like this neuters the utility, and makes the context switch happen rather arbitrarily.

For example, can't consider #35393's follow-up of reverting #35392 after this PR, because this would regress:

```lua
vim.o.ft = "lua"
vim.cmd "split|new"
vim.treesitter.stop(vim.fn.bufnr "#")
assert(vim.o.ft == "")
assert(vim.o.syntax == "")
```

Apologies - I should've marked my pr as a draft before the latest change.

(I believe) #35392 can be reverted according to the latest (more complex) changes.

please re-review when you have a moment

`apply_autocmds_group` is not that common of a function, let's just add a param to it. having many variants of a function can be really confusing.

separately, we should try to eliminate some of these params some day...

is this because modelines emit `OptionSet` ? ☹️ 

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/9881

Not quite. the modeline block needs buffer-specific context in order to just be correct here. now that the current buffer isn't always just 0.

consider if callbacks run with target as curbuf but do_modelines() runs after restoring the caller buffer, the autocmd part is fixed while the modeline part still mutates the wrong buffer.

trying to explain this as best as i can XD
done

ok. I wonder if we should just skip modelines when `buf` is given. Since (1) `buf` wasn't really doing anything useful before, and (2) modelines are somewhat deprecated, this may be worth constraining and documenting.

we have `vim._with()` so maybe `with_buf` is a good convention. or else `use_buf`

```suggestion
/// @param with_buf Run callbacks with "buf" as the current buffer
```

```suggestion
```

non-blocker: do we need tests for:

- An autocmd that wipes the target buffer during `nvim_exec_autocmds`
- An autocmd that changes `curbuf` during `nvim_exec_autocmds`
- A `nested = true` autocmd that itself triggers `nvim_exec_autocmds({buf=other})` (re-entrancy)

comment_regular_data.body: Looks like this causes some CI failures, do you need help fixing them?

Reworked. The buffer-context switch now only fires when the target has exactly one window in the current tab. Also, hidden and multi-window targets fall through to the pre-existing path.

NGL, while i ran like a billion local tests including strange scenarios, I would appreciate a second reviewer with someone especially knowledgeable on this sectoin of the code - not certain of who that'd be.

would it make sense to include the "followup" here, since it also serves to exercise real-world use-cases?


> cleanup `nvim_buf_call(self.bufnr` code like in [#35392 (comment)](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/35392#discussion_r2285614948)

there might be other cases, but idk

(missed that one, sorry). i don't directly apply the commits because I didn't know the behavior of the commit body/subject on merge with multiple commits... looking into it now sorry for the inconvenience.

EDIT: just fixed up the commits.

the `lintcommit` job can be ignored, it's informational

title: fix(api): make `nvim_exec_autocmds({ buf = ... })` use target buffer context
